Firstly, let there be $x$ women in the line. We now calculate the probability of the conditions being met. In order for $4$ or more men to be standing together, they must either be in one group of $4$ and one group of $2$, or one group of $6$. We consider the men to be in two units, one group of $4$ and one group of $2$. Note that when arranging the men into a line, if the two groups are separate, this accounts for the one group of $4$ and one group of $2$ possibility,and if they are adjacent, this accounts for the one group of $6$ possibility. We must now arrange $2$ groups of men and $x$ women into a line. The number of ways to do this is $\binom{x + 2}{2}$. However, note that for all arrangements in which the two groups are separate, there are $2$ ways to choose which group is the first in the line, and which is the second. We therefore multiply by $2$, to obtain $2\binom{x + 2}{2}$. However, this overcounts the arrangements in which the two groups are adjacent, i.e. they form a group of $6$, as when the two groups are adjacent, there is no way to distinguish which group is the first in the line, and which is second. We only want these arrangements to be counted for once . Since we multiplied our expression by $2$, we must then subtract the number of such arrangements. To calculate the number of arrangements in which the two groups are adjacent, we simply consider the two groups as one unit. We must now arrange one group of men and $x$ women into a line. The number of ways to do this is $\binom{x + 1}{1} = x + 1$. The total number of ways to arrange the men into a group of at least $4$ is then $2\binom{x + 2}{2} - (x + 1) = (x + 2)(x + 1) - (x + 1) = (x + 1)^2$. In order to find the probability, we must now calculate the total number of ways to arrange the men into a line according to the conditions of the problem. Note that the men can either be in two groups of $3$, or groups with an even number of men each (for this case we will use a strategy similar to the one above, by consider the men as groups of $2$). For the first case, we consider the groups of $3$ to be two individual units. We must now arrange two groups of men and $x$ women in a line. The number of ways to do this is $\binom{x + 2}{2}$. For the second case, we consider the groups of $2$ to be three individual units. We must now arrange $3$ groups of women and $x$ men in a line. The number of ways to do this is $\binom{x + 3}{3}$. However, in both of these cases, we are accounting for a group of $6$ men, i.e. when all of the smaller groups are adjacent. We are therefore double counting this instance, and must subtract the number of ways in which to arrange a group of $6$. As counted above, this is $x + 1$. The total number of ways in which to arrange the men is then $\binom{x + 2}{2} + \binom{x + 3}{3} - (x + 1) = (x + 1)\left(\dfrac{x + 2}{2} + \dfrac{(x + 3)(x + 2)}{6} - 1\right)$ $= (x + 1)\left(\dfrac{x}{2} + \dfrac{x^2 + 5x + 6}{6}\right) = (x + 1)\left(\dfrac{x^2 + 8x + 6}{6}\right)$. The desired probability is then $\dfrac{(x + 1)^2}{ (x + 1)\left(\dfrac{x^2 + 8x + 6}{6}\right)} = \dfrac{6x + 6}{x^2 + 8x + 6}$. From here we set this less than or equal to $\dfrac{1}{100}$, and it is easy to see that the least $x$ to satisfy this is $x = \boxed{594}$.

The AoPSWiki gives Denote (n) be n consecutive men and _ between (n) and (m) be some number of women between the men (possibly zero). There are five cases to consider: _(2)_(2)_(2)_ _(3)_(3)_ _(2)_(4)_ _(4)_(2)_ _(6)_ The first two cases give us all the possible ways to arrange the people. Let there be $n$ women. For the first case, if we think of (n) as dividers, we get $\dbinom{n+3}{3}$ ways. For the second case, we get $\dbinom{n+2}{2}$ cases. The third to fifth cases count the cases we desire. The third and fourth cases give us $2\dbinom{n+1}{2}$ if we put 1 woman between (2) and (4) before we count. the last case gives us $\dbinom{n+1}{1}$ so the probability is $\dfrac{ 2\dbinom{n+1}{2} + \dbinom{n+1}{1}}{\dbinom{n+3}{3}+\dbinom{n+2}{2}}$ the numerator simplifies to $(n+1)^2$. The denominator simplifies to $\dfrac{(n+6)(n+2)(n+1)}{6}$ so the whole faction simplifies to $\dfrac{6(n+1)}{(n+6)(n+2)}$ Since $\dfrac{n+1}{n+2}$ is slightly less than 1 when $n$ is large, $\dfrac{6}{n+6}$ will be close to $\dfrac{1}{100}$. They equal each other when $n = 594$. If we let $n= 595$ or $593$, we will notice that the answer is $\boxed{594}$ solution. Let $k$ be the number of women: $b_1,b_2, \cdots , b_k$. Denote the men as $a_1,a_2,a_3, \cdots , a_6$. $$\square \; b_1 \; \square \; b_2 \; \square \; \cdots \square \; b_k \; \square$$ We will set the man in squares. There are $k+1$ squares. Pick $3$ squares and $2$ men in each square: There are $\binom{k+1}{3} \cdot 6! \cdot k!$ ways to do that. Pick $2$ squares and $4$ men in one square, $2$ men in other square: There are $\binom{k+1}{2} \cdot 6! \cdot 2 \cdot k!$ ways. Pick $2$ squares and $3$ men in each square: $\binom{k+1}{2} \cdot 6! \cdot k!$ ways. Pick $1$ square and all $6$ men in each square: $\binom{k+1}{1} \cdot 6! \cdot k!$ ways. Thus, the probability that a group of at least four men stand together in the line is $$\frac{ \binom{k+1}{1}+ \binom{k+1}{2} \cdot 2}{\binom{k+1}{3}+ \binom{k+1}{2} \cdot 3+\binom{k+1}{1}} = \frac{6k+6}{k^2+8k+6}.$$ We need $$\frac{6k+6}{k^2+8k+6} \le \frac{1}{100} \iff k^2-592k-594 \ge 0 \iff k \ge 594.$$ Thus, the least number of women in the line is $594$.

Sorry to bump this, but if distinguishable vs indistinguishable truly doesn't matter, then why doesn't this solution work? There's $4$ cases, splitting up into $2, 2, 2; 2, 4; 3, 3;$ and just one group of $6$. Note that the number of ways to form each of these groups is $720$ since we're just splicing up a permutation of ${1,2,3,4,5,6}$ (for example, if our configuration was $2, 2, 2$, then the number of ways to form it is $\binom{6}{2} \cdot \binom{4}{2} \cdot 2^3$ (rearranging) which equals $720$). You can check that this actually works for the other cases. For the case $2, 2, 2$, we have a total of $w$ women plus these three groups, so there's $(w+3)!$ ways to arrange. Likewise, there's $(w+2)!, (w+2)!,$ and $(w+1)!$ ways to arrange for $2, 4; 3, 3;$ and just one group of 6 respectively. But then we overcounted some since $2, 2, 2$ could also end up being a $2, 4$ etc. The true count for $3, 3$ and $2,4$ ends up being $720 * ((w+2)! - (w+1)!)$ since we're just subtracting the cases where it turns out to be a group of $6$, and for $2, 2, 2$, it ends up being $720 * ((w+3)! - ((w+2)! - (w+1!)) - (w+1)!) = 720 * ((w+3)! - (w+2)!)$ Working out the division and setting up the inequality gives $097$, but I've got no clue why simply treating the men and women as distinguishable gave such a different result. author: AoPS
title: Suspended cube
problem_text: A cube with side length 10 is suspended above a plane. The vertex closest to the plane is labelled $A$. The three vertices adjacent to vertex $A$ are at heights 10, 11, and 12 above the plane. The distance from vertex $A$ to the plane can be expressed as $\tfrac{r-\sqrt{s}}{t}$, where $r$, $s$, and $t$ are positive integers, and $r+s+t<1000$. Find $r+s+t$.
solution_text:
Let the verticies of heights 10,11,12 be $C,B,D,$ respectively. Suppose $B=(0,0,11),C=(c,-d,10),D=(c,d,12)$ Notice the center of $BCD,$ say $O=(\frac{2c}{3},0,11).$ Vector $BC= ,BD= .$ Notice vector $OA$ is perpendicular to both these vectors, and has length $\frac{10\sqrt{3}} {3}.$ Thus, we can say $OA=k ,$ where, $pc-d-q=0, pc+d+q=0.$ We find $c=0,q=-d,$ so vecotr $OA=k .$ However, notice $|OA|=k\sqrt{1+d^2}=k\frac{CD} {2}=k\frac{10} {\sqrt{2}},$ so $k=\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}},$ so vector $OA= , z_A=11-d\frac{\sqrt{6}} {3}.$ So it remains to find $d.$ We know $\frac{CD}{2}=5\sqrt{2}=\sqrt{1+d^2},$ so $d=7.$ Hence the answer is $\frac{33-\sqrt{49*6}}{3}=\frac{33-\sqrt{294}} {3}\Rightarrow\boxed{330}$

Basically what we're doing is taking an equilateral triangle and projecting to the xy-plane. It's easy to find that the side lengths of the projected triangle are $\sqrt{199},\sqrt{199},14$, so we can set the coordinates of the points of the projected triangle to be $(7,0,0),(-7,0,0),(0,5\sqrt{6},0)$. Project them upwards according to the lengths of the perpendiculars to get the coordinates of the equilateral triangle. Now, we want to find the z-coordinate of the 4th vertex of the cube. We know the distance from the centroid of the equilateral triangle to this vertex, and so all we have to do is take the cross product of two vectors that define two sides of the triangle, scale by an appropriate amount, then add to the centroid to get the coordinates of the fourth vertex.

A quick solution without them, I think. Consider the equilateral triangle with side length 10rt2 formed by the three vertices close to A. It is tilted with the median from the 11-vertex being enitrely 11 above the plane, and the slope of the line between the 10-vertex and the 12-vertex is 1/7 (from pythag). From basic cube geometry, we know that the perpendicular line from A to the equilateral triangle's plane passes through the centroid of the equilateral triangle and has a length of 10rt3/3. Now we look at the right triangle whose hypotenuse is the line between the centroid and A and whose shorter leg is parallel to the plane in the problem. The ration of the legs is 7:1, because the hypotenuse is perpendicular to the line between the 10-vertex and the 12-vertex. Using similar triangles, we find that the 7-leg has length 7rt6/3. this length is the difference in height of A and the centroid above the plane. since the centroid is 11 above the plane, a is 11-(7rt6)/3 or (33-rt294)/3above the plane. so the answer is 33+294+3=330. this would be a lot easier to see with asymptote, sorry.

Let $A=(0,0,0)$ and the neighboring vertices be $(-10,0,0),(0,-10,0),(0,0,-10)$. Then the plane clearly has positive $x,y,z$-intercepts, whence its equation is $ax+by+cz-1=0$ for some $a,b,c>0$. By projections and the fact that distances are nonnegative, where $r=\sqrt{a^2+b^2+c^2}$ and $d$ is the distance from $A$ to the plane, \begin{align*} d &= \frac{1}{r}\\ 10 &= \frac{|-10a-1|}{r} = \frac{10a+1}{r} \implies 10r-1 = 10a\\ 11 &= \frac{10b+1}{r} \implies 11r-1 = 10b\\ 12 &= \frac{10c+1}{r} \implies 12r-1 = 10c, \end{align*}so squaring the last three equations and adding, we have \ But \ and \

Fast solution (does not use vectors). We have an equilateral triangle ABC of side length 10sqrt2 that is flat on the XY plane at height 11 (height measured in Z axis). Let M be the centroid of ABC and X the fourth vertex. At this point, X is 10/sqrt3 below 11 (XM =10/sqrt3 through simple geometry). Now, rotate the triangle about the axis AD where D is midpoint of BC so that B is height 12 and C is height 10. Since we have rotated around an axis that contains M, we can calculate the Z-axis distance of MX by multiplying MX by cos(angle of rotation). Looking at triangle formed by B, C and the point on z=10 (the height of C) right below B, we have hypotenuse 10sqrt2 and height 2. Thus the cosine value we want is 14/10sqrt2 = 7/5sqrt2 11- (10/sqrt3 x 7/5sqrt2) gives our desired answer. Let vertex $A$ be at a point $(0,0,t)$. Let us assume the vertex with heights $10,11,12$ are $B,C,D$. One can achieve any configuration of the cube by simply rotating it in the $y-z$ plane by an angle $\theta$ and in the $x-y$ plane by an angle $\phi$. Let us let our initial cube was parallel to the $x-y$ plane (we are only asked to find $t$ so the initial configuration in the $xy$ plane does not matter). Then from trigonometry, we get $B(10\cos{\theta},0,10),C(0,10\sin{\phi},11),D(10\sin{\theta},10\cos{\phi},12)$. Using $AB=AC=AD=10$, we end up getting the equation $$100+(10-t)^2+(11-t)^2+(12-t)^2=200$$ (which we like since it is symmetric.) This gives us $$3t^2-66t+265$$ which gives $t=\frac{33-\sqrt{294}}{3}$ for an answer of $33+3+294=\boxed{330}$.

Distances mean analytic geometry! As in 2021 AIME I #6, we let $A$ be the origin and the three vertices of the cube nearest to $A$ be $B=(10, 0, 0), C=(0, 10, 0),$ and $D=(0, 0, 10)$. Suppose plane $\mathcal{P}$ has equation $ap+bq+cr+d=0$ for real numbers $a, b, c,$ and $d,$ and choose these numbers so that $\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}}=1$. From the condition that $\overrightarrow{B\mathcal{P}}=10, \overrightarrow{C\mathcal{P}}=11,$ and $\overrightarrow{D\mathcal{P}}=12$ we get, from the point-to-plane distance formula, \begin{align*} \dfrac{10a+0b+0c+d}{\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}}}=10 \\ \dfrac{0a+10b+0c+d}{\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}}}=11 \\ \dfrac{0a+0b+10c+d}{\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}}}=12 \\ \end{align*} These simplify to \begin{align*} 10a+d=10 \\ 10b+d=11 \\ 10c+d=12 \\ \end{align*} since $a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}=1$. We want to find $\overrightarrow{A\mathcal{P}},$ which equals $\dfrac{d}{\sqrt{a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}}}=d$. Rearranging the above system of equations gives \begin{align*} 10a=10-d \\ 10b=11-d \\ 10c=12-d \\ \end{align*} Squaring each of these results in \begin{align*} 100a^{2}=(10-d)^{2} \\ 100b^{2}=(11-d)^{2} \\ 100c^{2}=(12-d)^{2} \\ \end{align*} Add the three equations to get \ since $a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}=1$. Substitute $(11-d)^{2}=f$ to get $3f+2=100,$ or $f=\tfrac{98}{3}$. Then $d=11-\sqrt{f}=11-\sqrt{\tfrac{98}{3}}=\tfrac{33-\sqrt{294}}{3},$ and the requested sum is $33+294+3=\boxed{330}$.

Let $X$, $Y$, and $Z$ be the vertices adjacent to $A$ with heights $10$, $11$, and $12$, respectively. Let the feet of the perpendiculars from $X$, $Y$, and $Z$ onto the plane be $D$, $E$, and $F$, respectively. Taking the cross section including $X$, $Y$, $D$, and $E$, we apply the Pythagorean Theorem: $DE^2 + (11-10)^2 = XY^2 = 200 \implies DE = \sqrt{199}$. Similarly, $EF=\sqrt{199}$ and $FD=14$. If $M$ is the midpoint of $DF$, then the Pythagorean Theorem gives $EM=5\sqrt6$. Let the plane be $z=0$, and let $M$ be the origin. Then let $X=(-7,0,10)$, $Y=(0,5\sqrt6,11)$, $Z=(7,0,12)$. If $A=(x,y,z)$, we have $$(x+7)^2+y^2+(z-10)^2=100 \qquad\qquad (1)$$ $$(x-7)^2+y^2+(z-12)^2=100\qquad\qquad (2)$$ $$x^2+(y-5\sqrt6)^2+(z-11)^2=100\qquad\qquad (3)$$ The average of $(1)$ and $(2)$ is $x^2+49+y^2+z^2-22z+122=100$. Subtracting $(3)$ from this gives $$49+10y\sqrt6-150+122-121=0$$ $$10y\sqrt6=100$$ $$y=\frac{10}{\sqrt6}=\frac{5\sqrt6}{3}. \qquad (4)$$ Subtracting $(2)$ from $(1)$ gives $$28x+4z-44=0\implies z-11=-7x \qquad (5)$$ Substituting $(5)$ and $(4)$ into $(3)$, we get $$x^2+\left( \frac{5\sqrt6}{3}-5\sqrt6\right)^2+(7x)^2=100$$ $$50x^2+\frac{200}{3}=100$$ $$x^2=\frac23$$ $$x=\frac{\sqrt6}{3}$$ Finally, from $(5)$ we have $z=11-7x=11-\frac{7\sqrt6}{3}=\frac{33-\sqrt{294}}{3}\implies \boxed{330}$.

Rotate so that the four vertices we care about are $A = (0, 0, 0), (10, 0, 0), (0, 10, 0), (0, 0 ,10)$. Let the plane $\mathcal{P}$ be $ax + by + cz + d = 0$, with coefficients normalized such that $a^2 + b^2 + c^2 = 1$. Then the distance from $(x_0, y_0, z_0)$ to $\mathcal{P}$ is $\frac{ax_0 + by_0 + cz_0 + d}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2 + c ^2}} = ax_0 + by_0 + cz_0 + d$. \\ WLOG, we have \begin{align*} 10a + d &= 10 \\ 10b + d &= 11 \\ 10c + d &= 12 \end{align*} and want to find $d$. We see that $10(a + b + c) = 33-3d$, and by pairwise multiplying the equations and adding, $100(ab + ac + bc) = 362 - 3d^2 - 20d(a+b+c) = 3d^2 - 66d + 362$. \\ Now $a^2 + b^2 + c^2 = 1 \iff (10(a+b+c))^2 - 200(ab+ac+bc) = 100$, which simplifies to a quadratic in $d$. Solve $3d^2 - 66d + 265 = 0$ and extract $\boxed{330}$.

author: AoPS
title: Three-digit numbers divisible by 4
problem_text: Find the number of positive integers with three not necessarily distinct digits, $abc$, with $a \neq 0$, $c \neq 0$ such that both $abc$ and $cba$ are divisible by 4.
solution_text:
Note that $a$ and $c$ are even. Given any $b$, we have two choices for each $a$ and $c$ (if b is even then a and c are 0 mod 4 and if b is odd then a and c are 2 mod 4). Thus we get in total $10 \cdot 2 \cdot 2 = \boxed{040}$>

A number is divisible by four if its last two digits are divisible by 4. Thus, we require that $ 10b + a $ and $ 10b + c$ are both divisible by $ 4 $. If $ b $ is odd, then $ a $ and $ c $ must both be $ 2 \pmod 4 $ meaning that $ a $ and $ c $ are $ 2 $ or $ 6 $. If $ b $ is even, then $ a $ and $ c $ must be $ 0 \pmod 4 $ meaning that $ a $ and $ c $ are $ 4 $ or $ 8 $. Case 2: $b\equiv 1\pmod{2}$. This implies $a,c\equiv 2\pmod{4}$, which implies $a,c\in \{2,6\}$. If $2b \equiv 0 \pmod{4}$, $b$ must be even, resulting in five possibilities for $b$. In this case, $a$ and $c$ also must be a multiple of $4$, which results in $2$ possibilities for each $a$ and $c$ (because they are both leading digits). This case totals to $5 \cdot 2 \cdot 2 = 20$ possible three digit positive integers. If $2b \equiv 2 \pmod{4}$, then $b$ must be odd, also resulting in five possibilities for $b$. $a$ and $c$ also must be $2 \pmod{4}$ to make $2b+a$ and $2b+c$ multiples of 4. There are also two possibilities for $a$ and $c$, so the total number of possibilities in this case is also $5 \cdot 2 \cdot 2 = 20$ Adding these up gives a total of $20 + 20 = \boxed{040}$

Both $\overline{bc}$ and $\overline{ba}$ are divisible by $4$. Case 1: $a\equiv c\equiv 0\pmod 4$. Then there are $4$ ways to choose $a$ and $c$. Since $b$ is even, there are $5$ ways to choose $b$. $20$ total ways. Case 2: $a\equiv c\equiv 2\pmod 4$ Then there are $4$ ways to choose $a$ and $c$. Since $b$ must be odd, then there are $5$ ways to choose $b$. $20$ total ways. Anser is $20+20=\boxed{040}$.

author: AoPS
title: An arithmetic sequence with terms increased
problem_text: The terms of an arithmetic sequence add to $715$. The first term of the sequence is increased by $1$, the second term is increased by $3$, the third term is increased by $5$, and in general, the $k$th term is increased by the $k$th odd positive integer. The terms of the new sequence add to $836$. Find the sum of the first, last, and middle terms of the original sequence.
solution_text:
The sum of the first series can be written as $ n(a_1 + a_n)/2 $ Instead of trying to add 1 to the first term, 3 to the second, etc, just write the new series as $ n(a_1 + a_n)/2) + n^2 $, because $n^2$ is the sum of the first n odd integers. The two series added is equal to $836$ and the first is equal to $715$. By subtracting the old series from the new, we find that $n = 11$. Plug that into the first equation to get $11/2(a_1 + a_n) = 715 $ and solve to find $(a_1 +a_n) = 130$. The middle term is the average of the first and last, so $130 + 130/2 = 195$

aww... sniped Let the sequence be defined as $a + a+r + a+2r ... + a + (n-1)r$ where n is the number of terms in the sequence. We have $na + \frac{(n-1)(n)}{2} r = 715$ and $na + \frac{(n-1)(n)}{2} r + n^2 = 836$. Adding 121 to the first equation yields $na + \frac{(n-1)(n)}{2} r + 121 = 836$. $na + \frac{(n-1)(n)}{2} r + 121 = na + \frac{(n-1)(n)}{2} r + n^2$. We are left with $121 = n^2 \implies n = 11$. We now know there are 11 terms in the sequence. By substitution, we have $11a + 55r = 715$. Dividing by 11 yields $a + 5r = 65$. $a+5r$ is the middle term of the sequence. Note that the arithmetic mean of the first and last terms of the sequence is equivalent to the value of the middle term. Therefore, the sum of the first and last terms is $65 \times 2 = 130$. The sum of the first, middle, and last term is $130 + 65$ which is $\boxed{195}$.

We are given that $a_1+a_2+...+a_n=715$ Also, $a_1+(a_2+1)+(a_3+3)+...+(a_n+2n-1)$ $=a_1+...+a_n+(1+...+2n-1)=715+n^2=836 \rightarrow n=11$ We want to find $a_1+a_6+a_{11}=a_1+(a_1+5d)+(a_1+10d)=3a_1+15d=3(a_1+5d)$. $a_1+a_2+...+a_n$ $=a_1+(a_1+d)+...+(a_1+d)$ $=11a_1+(1+...+10)d$ $=11a_1+55d$ $=11(a_1+5d)=715$. So $a_1+5d=65$ And our answer is three times this quantity! $65\times 3=\boxed{195}$

Assume there are $k$ terms in the sequence. Let them be $a_1, a_2, a_3, \cdots a_k$. The sum of the original sequence is $715$, therefore $\sum_{i=1}^{k}a_i=715$. Also, $\sum_{i=1}^{k}a_i+\sum_{i=1}^{k}(2i-1)=836$ from when we increase all the terms. Therefore, we get $715+\sum_{i=1}^{k}(2i-1)=836\implies 715+k^2=836\implies k=11$. The sum of the original sequence is $\frac{11(a_1+a_{11})}{2}=715$. From this, we get $a_1+a_{11}=65*2$. Now, note that $a_6=\frac{a_1+a_{11}}{2}$ by definition of an arithmetic sequence, and we get $a_1+a_{11}+a_6=(a_1+a_{11})(\frac32)=65*2*\frac32=\boxed{195}$.

The terms of an arithmetic sequence add to $715$. The first term of the sequence is increased by $1$, the second term is increased by $3$, the third term is increased by $5$, and in general, the $k$th term is increased by the $k$th odd positive integer. The terms of the new sequence add to $836$. Find the sum of the first, last, and middle terms of the original sequence. After the adding of the odd numbers, the total of the sequence increases by $836 - 715 = 121 = 11^2$. Since the sum of the first $11$ positive odd numbers is $11^2$, there must be $11$ terms in the sequence, so the mean of the sequence is $\dfrac{715}{11} = 65$. Since the first, last, and middle terms are centered around the mean, our final answer is $65 \cdot 3 = \boxed{195}$

sum of first $n$ odd numbers is $n^2$ so we find that there are 11 terms in the sequence. now we need to find $a+(a+5d)+(a+10d)=(a+5d)+(2a+10d)$ and letting $a+5d=x$ we find $x=65$ (since $\frac{11(2a+10d)}{2}=715$) so $3x=195$.

The total sum after adding the odd integers would increase by $k^2$. So, $k = \sqrt{836-715} = \sqrt{121} = 11$. Therefore, there are $11$ terms in our partial arithmetic sequence. The sum of the first, middle, and last terms of the original sequence that we want to find is $a_1 + \frac{a_1 + a_k}{2} + a_k = 3\left(\frac{a_1 + a_k}{2}\right)$. But, we know that $\frac{a_1 + a_k}{2} \cdot 11 = 715$ by the sum of a partial arithmetic sequence formula. This means that $\frac{a_1 + a_k}{2} = \frac{715}{11} = 65$. Therefore, the sum is $3 \cdot 65 = \boxed{195}$.

p3: We have $836-715 = 121$, so we must've increased up to the 11th odd positive integer and so there are $11$ terms. The middle term is $\frac{715}{11} = 65$, and the sum of the first and last terms is $65 \cdot 2$, so the answer is $65 \cdot 3 = \boxed{195}$.

Let the arithmetic sequence be $a, a + d, \dots$. Then if there are $n$ terms we are adding $n^2$. Then $n^2 = 121 \implies n = 11$. Then $(a + 5d) \cdot 11 = 715$. We want to find $a + 10d + a + 5d + a = 3a + 15d$ so answer extracting we have $3 \cdot \frac{715}{11} = 195$.

Let the arithmetic sequence be $a_1 + a_2 + a_3 + \dots + a_n = 715$. We also have $(a_1+1) + (a_2 + 3) + (a_3 + 5) + (a_n+(2n-1)) = 836$. Notice that the first arithmetic sequence is in the second arithmetic sequence. We can rewrite the second arithmetic sequence as \ Which is also $715 + (1+3+5+\dots+(2n-1))=836$. Note that the sum $(1+3+5+\dots+(2n-1))=n^2$. Therefore we have $n^2 = 836-715 = 121$. We now have $n=11$. Now we are looking for the sum of $a_1+a_6+a_{11}$. We know that $a_6$ will be the average of $a_1$ and $a_{11}$, so from that we know that $a_1+a_{11} = 2a_6$. Now if we continue the average of $a_2$ and $a_{10}$ will also be $a_6$ so the sum of $a_2$ and $a_{10}$ is $2a_6$. If we continue this, we should see that the sum of $a_1 + a_2 + a_3 + \dots + a_{11} = 735$ will just be equivalent to $11a_6 = 735$. Note that $a_1+a_{11}+a_6 = 2a_6 + a_6 = 3a_6$, so we can just multiply $735$ by $11a_6=735$ by $\frac{3}{11}$ to get $3a_6=715\left(\frac{3}{11}\right) = \boxed{195}$.

author: AoPS
title: Ordering and serving meals randomly
problem_text: Nine people sit down for dinner where there are three choices of meals. Three people order the beef meal, three order the chicken meal, and three order the fish meal. The waiter serves the nine meals in random order. Find the number of ways in which the waiter could serve the meal types to the nine people such that exactly one person receives the type of meal ordered by that person.
solution_text:
Well, I got 216. Anyone else? Edit: Oh, the answer key is up already. Here is my solution: CCCBBBFFF There are 9 ways to pick the person with the correct meal. So WLOG, let this be person one who ordered chicken. We now have: CCCBBBFFF C________ Now, we wish to place the two other C's. There are 3 cases to consider: Case 1: Both go to B's CCCBBBFFF CFFCCFBBB There are 3 ways to arrange the two C's . The F's must be given to person two, three, and the remaining B. Case 2: Both go to F's This is the same as case 1 and yields 3 ways also. Case 3: One goes to B and the other goes to F CCCBBBFFF C__CFFCBB There are 3*3 ways to place the 2 C's. There are 2 ways to place the remaining F and B, for a total of 3*3*2 ways. Adding this together, we have 9(3+3+3*3*2)= 216

Each F must get B, so this has only 1 possibility, making 3*1 cases III. 1 goes to B 2 go to F there are 2*3=6 ways for this case We are left with B CCC F and bb fff. we know F gets b and B gets F, so we are left with CCC and bff. these have 3 cases, and 6*3=18, so 18 for this entire case So total possibilities is 9(3+3+18)=216

First, there are nine people we can choose from to receive the correct dish. For the sake of explaining, let it be a chicken person. I drew a diagram on the test that looked like this so I could kind of visualize things. $C$ $C$ $C$ $B$ $B$ $B$ $F$ $F$ $F$ $\surd X \, X \, X \, X \, X \, X X X $ After assuming the chicken person got the correct dish, I broke it up into two cases: Case 1 : The two other chicken people got the same dish. There are two ways to choose this dish (beef or fish). Let's let that dish be beef. Then, there are three fish dishes remaining, one beef, and two chicken dishes remaining. The three beef guys then must get the three fish dishes, since the fish people can't be served the dish they wanted. Then, between the fish people, two will get chicken, and one will get beef. There are three ways to choose who gets the chicken. So there are a total of $2 \times 3 = 6$ possible cases here. Case 2 : The two other chicken people get different dishes. So one will get beef and one will get fish. There are two ways to choose who gets beef and who gets fish. There are two chicken dishes, two beef dishes, and two fish dishes remaining. The fish dishes must go to the beef people, and the beef dishes must go to the fish people. So of the three beef people, one will get chicken and two will get fish. There are three ways to choose who gets the chicken. Likewise, with the fish people, one will get chicken and two will get beef. There are three ways to choose that as well. So we have $2 \times 3 \times 3 = 18$ total cases with this one. Add the two up and we have $18 + 6 = 24$. Since there are $9$ people we can choose to receive the correct dish, we have $24 \times 9 = \boxed{216}$.

For the people that order 2B, they can get one of the following: (C/C, C/F, F/C, F/F) Case 1: $C/C$ or $F/F$ WLOG assume that it is $C/C$ and multiply by 2 at end of case to account for getting $F/F$. Food orders: 3C, 3F Food left to give: 2B, C, 3F The people that ordered chicken must be given fish, because if they won't then the fish will go to a fish person, and we'll get two people with fish. Then, we have the following: Food orders: 3F Food left to give: 2B, C We have $\binom{3}{1}=3$ ways to choose which person gets fish, and the total count for $\binom{3}{1}*2=6$. Case 2: $C/F$ or $F/C$ Again, WLOG assume that it is $C/F$, and multiply by $2$. We get: Food left: 2B, 2C, 2F Orders: 3C, 3F For people who ordered chicken, we could have $(2B/F)$ or $(B/2F)$. The first case gives us $2C, F$ to give to people who ordered fish, however, this would give us one person having fish, which would give us a total of $2$ people getting the correct order, contradiction. In the second case, we have $\binom{3}{1}=3$ ways to choose who gets the beef. Then, we get the following: Food left: B, 2C Orders: 3F We have $\binom{3}{1}=1$ way to choose which person that ordered fish gets beef, and overall we get $\binom{3}{1}*\binom{3}{1}=9$ ways for $C/F$. Multiplying by $2$ gives $9*2=18$. Adding case 1/case 2 gives $6+18=24$. Times 9 from beginning give $24*9=\boxed{216}$.

WLOG 1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9 oreders a,a,a,b,b,b,c,c,c respectively. Choose the one who receives his own meal: 9C1 WLOG 1 receives a. Then the rest is, the coefficient of $ a^2 b^3 c^3 $ of $ (b+c)^2 (c+a)^3 (a+b)^3 =$ $ (a^3 +3a^2 b+3ab^2 +b^3) $ $ (b^2 +2bc+c^2)$ $ (c^3 +3c^2 a+3ca^2 +a^3)$ You can see easily the terms of $a^2 b^3 c^3$ : $ (3a^2 b)(b^2)(c^3) + (3ab^2)(2bc)(3c^2 a) + (b^3)(c^2)(3ca^2) $ The coefficient is $ 3+18+3 = 24 $. So the answer is $ (9) (24) = 216 $

Nine people sit down for dinner where there are three choices of meals. Three people order the beef meal, three order the chicken meal, and three order the fish meal. The waiter serves the nine meals in random order. Find the number of ways in which the waiter could serve the meal types to the nine people such that exactly one person receives the type of meal ordered by that person. We will split it into cases: Case 1: One of the people who ordered beef got the correct meal Case 1.1: The two people who got the other two beef meals both ordered fish Case 1.2: The two people who got the other two beef meals ordered fish and chicken Case 1.3: The two people who got the other two beef meals both ordered chicken Note that the corresponding Case 2 and Case 3 are equivalent, so we can multiply by three at the end. In Case 1, there are three ways to choose which person got the correct meal. In Case 1.1, there are $\dbinom32 = 3$ ways to choose which two people got the beef meals. Now we know that the three people who ordered chicken got fish meals, because there are no more beef meals. The remaining chicken meals go to the remaining people. Case 1.3 is equivalent to this In Case 1.2, there are $3 \cdot 3 = 9$ ways to choose which person who ordered fish and which person who ordered chicken got the beef meals. Now the remaining two people who ordered chicken must get fish meals, and the remaining two people who ordered fish must get chicken meals. There are then two ways to choose which one out of the remaining people who ordered beef gets the remaining chicken meal (and the last person gets the remaining fish meal) Therefore, we have a total of $3(3 \cdot 2 + 3 \cdot 3 \ cdot 2) = 72$ ways in Case 1, so we have $3 \cdot 72 = \boxed{216}$ ways in total.

They can pick only chicken chicken chicken (1 arrangement, 1 way to finish) for $1 \cdot 1 = 1$ way. Multiplying by $3$ to account for arrangements gives $1 \cdot 3 = 3$ ways. WLOG the person who received the correct meal ordered a beef meal. We'll multiply by $3$ at the end. Let's also consider the people who ordered a chicken meal first. There are now three cases as to how many of each meal is left. Case 1: 2 beef, 3 chicken, 0 fish. Now let's have the fish people choose. They can pick beef beef chicken (3 arrangements, 1 way to finish) beef chicken chicken (3 arrangements, 0 ways to finish since we have a beef and a chicken & 2 people and we are not allowed to choose beef, so impossible) and chicken chicken chicken (1 arrangement, 0 ways to finish since we have 2 beefs & 2 people and we are not allowed to choose beef, so impossible). This gives $3 \cdot 1 = 3$ ways. Case 2: 1 beef, 3 chicken, 1 fish. Now let's have the fish people choose. They can pick beef chicken chicken (3 arrangements, 2 ways to finish) or chicken chicken chicken (3 arrangements, 0 ways to finish) for $3 \cdot 2 = 6$ ways. Case 3: 0 beef, 3 chicken, 2 fish. Now let's have the fish people choose. They can pick only chicken chicken chicken (1 arrangement, 1 way to finish) for $1 \cdot 1 = 1$ way. That's a total of $10$ ways. author: AoPS
title: Running away from a place
problem_text: Butch and Sundance need to get out of Dodge. To travel as quickly as possible, each alternates walking and riding their only horse, Sparky, as follows. Butch begins walking as Sundance rides. When Sundance reaches the first of their hitching posts that are conveniently located at one-mile intervals along their route, he ties Sparky to the post and begins walking. When Butch reaches Sparky, he rides until he passes Sundance, then leaves Sparky at the next hitching post and resumes walking, and they continue in this manner. Sparky, Butch, and Sundance walk at 6, 4, and 2.5 miles per hour, respectively. The first time Butch and Sundance meet at a milepost, they are $n$ miles from Dodge, and have been traveling for $t$ minutes. Find $n + t$.

We can set up a system of equations. Butch and Sundance spend their entire time either walking or riding. Let B be the number of miles that Butch walks, b be the number of miles that he rides, S be the number of miles that Sundance walks, and s be the number of miles that Sundance rides. Since Butch and Sundance travel the same distance, we get that B+b=S+s We van also find that to travel 1 mile, it takes Butch 15 minutes, Sundance 24 minutes, and Sparky 10 minutes. Since Butch and Sundance never stop, they must have traveled for the same amount of time, or 15B+10b=24S+10s Multiplying the first equation by 10 and subtracting, we get 5B=14S The smallest positive integral values of B and S that satisfy the equation are B=14 and S=5, so Butch walks for 14 miles and Sundance walks for 5, a total of 19 miles. Plugging these values into the second equation (given that Butch and Sundance must be riding when they are not walking), we find that they spent 260 minutes, for and answer of 260+19=279.

I didn't take the AIME, but I found a nice solution after bashing it out at home: We convert the rates to time (currency) that has to be elapsed (spent) to travel one mile. Either it takes one person 24 minutes and the other 10 minutes, or it takes one person 10 minutes and another person 24 minutes. If we fix our point of view, we can look at each mile as a way to catch up by gaining 14 minutes, or fall behind by losing 5 minutes. Therefore, 5x - 14y = 0. We observe that after 0 miles, they cannot meet up until (x,y) = (14,5) --> 19 miles. from our point of view, this is 14 ten minute periods and 5 24 minute periods. It follows that the answer is 260 +19= 279

author: AoPS
title: Binary integers whose difference is 1
problem_text: Let $B$ be the set of all binary integers that can be written using exactly 5 zeros and 8 ones where leading zeros are allowed. If all possible subtractions are performed in which one element of $B$ is subtracted from another, find the number of times the answer 1 is obtained.
solution_text:
Another way to read the problem is to see how many integers in B you can subtract 1 from and get another integer in B. Thus, the act of subtracting 1 must not change the number of 0's or 1's in the result. Case 1: Integer ending in 1 Subtract 1 and you get a number ending in 0. Case 2: Integer ending in 10 Subtract 1 and you get a number ending in 01. Case 3: Integer ending in 100 Subtract 1 and you get a number ending in 011. Case 4...n: Integer ending in 10...0 Subtract 1 and you get a number ending in 01...1 Only case 2 produces a valid result. The number of integers corresponding to case 2 are numbers beginning with 7 ones and 4 zeros in some order and ending in 10. So, the answer is C(11,7)=330.

Just note that the first two digits from the right are $01$. That gives $330$ such pairs.

Let's look at the last two digits. The only way to subtract and get a one is for the last two digits to be 11 and 10, or 10 and 01. Since the rest of the digits have to be the same, the former case is impossible, leaving us only with the latter. Therefore, there's eleven slots before the last two digits, with seven ones and 4 zeroes. The number of arrangements of these numbers is 11C7, so The answer is $ 11!/(7!*4!) = 330 $

Notice that there can only be an equal number of 0's and 1's if the larger number is ...10 and the smaller number is ...01. Thus, there are 7 1's and 4 0's, so the answer is 11C4=330.

The only preservation of equal number of 0s and 1s is if the last 2 digits are 01 and 10. We have to account for 11 other digits, 4 of which are 0s and 7 are 1s; $\binom{11}{4} = \boxed{330}$

First note that it is impossible to just have the two numbers end in a $1$ and a $0$. This is because we have an uneven number of $1's$ and $0's$ remaining, and they can not cancel each other out to make $0$. Thus, we need to add constraints to these numbers in order to achieve the task. Next note that $10_2-1_2=1_2$, so an option is to force the two numbers to end in $10$ and $01$. This works, because now we have an even number of zeros and ones distributed among the remaining digits of the two binary integers. Thus, when we place the integers as explained, we have $4$ 0s and $7$ 1s to distribute among the remaining $11$ digits of either number. (We say either because we we will then place the digits of the other integer to match up with the digits of the first integer) Thus we can say that we are choosing $4$ places out of $11$ spots to put the four $1's$ we need to complete the requirements, and we have $\displaystyle\binom{11}{4}=\boxed{330}$ ways to arrange the digits. An example of two integers that work is \ Notice that $10$ and $01$ are the last two digits for these integers, and all the other digits match up so that they cancel when subtracted.

Let $B$ be the set of all binary integers that can be written using exactly 5 zeros and 8 ones where leading zeros are allowed. If all possible subtractions are performed in which one element of $B$ is subtracted from another, find the number of times the answer 1 is obtained. There are four possible endings to a binary number: $00$, $01$, $10$, and $11$. It is clear that the first 11 digits of two numbers in $B$ have to be the same in order to preserve the number of $0$s and $1$s, and to make the difference $0$. Therefore, if two numbers in $B$ have a difference of $1$, then their last two digits should also have a difference of $1$. The only pair of digits in the possible endings that preserves the number of $0$s and $1$s and also has a difference of $1$ is $01$ and $10$. Therefore, the problem is just making up the rest of the number, which can be done in $\dbinom{11}{4} = \boxed{330}$.

consider the binary numbers $a$ and $b$ such that $a,b\in{B}$. we are given the requirement that they each have exactly 5 zeroes and 8 ones and also $a-b=1\Leftrightarrow{a=b+1}$ (here we assumed WLOG that a>b). Thus we are looking for $a,b\in{B}$ such that adding $1$ to $b$ produces another element of $B$. Experimenting with possible endings of a binary number we find that only $01$ works otherwise the balance of 5 zeroes and 8 ones will be disrupted. This leaves us with pairs $a,b$ such that $b$ ends in $01$, and since leading zeroes are allowed, we have 4 zeroes and 7 ones left to place anywhere on 11 empty spaces, i.e. ${{11}\choose{4}}=\boxed{330}$ such numbers $b\in{B}$.

Solution: Notice that, for a possible subtraction of two elements of $B$ to result in $1$, one element has to end in $0$ and the other in $1$. (This is because if they both ended in the same digit, then the rightmost digit of the result would be $0$.) Furthermore, this means that the rest of the digits of these said elements of $B$ have to match up except for one pair of which one will be $1$ and one will be $0$. However, this one pair has to be the second rightmost digits to result in an answer of $1$. So, our two elements will be: ...10 and ...01, where indicates a set of four zeros and seven ones. There are $\frac{11!}{7! \cdot 4!} = \boxed{330}$ possible values of , and hence that is the number of times the answer $1$ is obtained.

Note that this only occurs if the last two digits are $10$. Then we use stars and bars to arrange remaining $0$'s and $1$'s to get an answer of $\binom{11}{7} = 330$.

author: AoPS
title: Imaginary part of a complex number
problem_text: The complex numbers $z$ and $w$ satisfy $z^{13} = w$, $w^{11} = z$, and the imaginary part of $z$ is $\sin\left(\frac{m\pi}n\right)$ for relatively prime positive integers $m$ and $n$ with $m < n$. Find $n$.
solution_text:
Note that $w^{143}=w$ and similar for $z$, and they are not equal to $0$ because the question implies the imaginary part is positive. Thus $w^{142}=z^{142}=1$, so each is of the form $sin(2*pi*k/142)$ where $k$ is a positive integer between $0$ and $141$ inclusive. This simplifies to $sin(pi*k/71)$, and $071$ is prime, so it is the only possible denominator, and thus is the answer.

Solution: By substitution, $z^{143} = z$. Let $z = r\text{cis}\theta$. Hence, $r^{143}\text{cis}(143\theta) = r\text{cis}\theta \Rightarrow r^{143} = r \Rightarrow \bf{r=1},$ $143\theta = \theta + 2k\pi \Rightarrow \bf{\theta = \frac{k\pi}{71}}$. So, $z = \cos\left(\frac{k\pi}{71}\right) + i\sin\left(\frac{k\pi}{71}\right) \Rightarrow \boxed{071}$.

Replace $w$ in the second equation with $z^{13}$. Then we have that $z^{143} = z$. Dividing both sides by $z$ gives us $z^{142} = z$. Finding the real and imaginary parts of z in terms of $sin$ and $cos$ are trivial with the roots of unity. By roots of unity, the solutions to $z^{n} = 1$ are in the form of $\operatorname{cis}\left(\frac{2\pi k}n\right)$. Thus, plugging in $142$ for $n$ gives us $\operatorname{cis}\left(\frac{\pi k}{71}\right)$. $\operatorname{Cis}\left(\frac{\pi k}{71}\right)$ is equivalent to $\cos \left(\frac{\pi k}{71}\right) + i\sin \left(\frac{\pi k}{71} \right)$. Finally, the imaginary part is $\sin \left(\frac{\pi k}{71} \right)$. Since we are looking only for $n$, our answer is $\boxed{071}$. It's important to note that $71$ is prime, so $n$ has to be $71$.

Note that this implies $z^{143} = z$ or $z^{142} = 1$. Then $z$ is a $142$nd root of unity. Thus $z = \text{cis}\left(\frac{2\pi m}{142} \right) = \text{cis}\left(\frac{\pi m}{71} \right)$ Now note that $71$ is prime. So from $m < n$ we can conclude that $\gcd(m, 71) = 1$ and hence $n = 71$.

author: AoPS
title: Trading coins
problem_text: At each of the sixteen circles in the network below stands a student. A total of 3360 coins are distributed among the sixteen students. All at once, all students give away all their coins by passing an equal number of coins to each of their neighbors in the network. After the trade, all students have the same number of coins as they started with. Find the number of coins the student standing at the center circle had originally. import graph; unitsize(1 cm); pair[] O; O = (0,0); O = 0.6*dir(270); O = 0.6*dir(270 + 360/5); O = 0.6*dir(270 + 2*360/5); O = 0.6*dir(270 + 3*360/5); O = 0.6*dir(270 + 4*360/5); O = 1.2*dir(90); O = 1.2*dir(90 + 360/5); O = 1.2*dir(90 + 2*360/5); O = 1.2*dir(90 + 3*360/5); O = 1.2*dir(90 + 4*360/5); O = 2*dir(270); O = 2*dir(270 + 360/5); O = 2*dir(270 + 2*360/5); O = 2*dir(270 + 3*360/5); O = 2*dir(270 + 4*360/5); draw(O --O ); draw(O --O ); draw(O --O ); draw(O --O ); draw(O --O ); draw(O --O --O --O --O --O --O --O --O --O --cycle); draw(O --O --O --O --O --O --O --O --O --O --cycle); draw(O --O --O --O --O --cycle); for(int i = 1; i <= 16; ++i) { filldraw(Circle(O ,0.2),white,black); }
solution_text:
Even though it is incredibly intuitive to say that the number of coins held is symmetrical, I avoided assuming it and came up with this: Let the center student have $5x$ coins. Each of the 5 people "connected" to him gives him 1/3 of their coins, so the ring of 5 people around him must have a total of $15x$ coins. That ring is "down" $10x$ coins, which they must receive from the second ring; half of the second ring's coins go to the first ring, so the second ring must have $20x$ coins. Similarly, the outer ring has $20x$ coins. $60x = 3360$ and so $5x = 280.$

Assuming all the students gave an certain number of coins with their neighbors, and this certain number was the same for all the givings from all the students, this certain amount of coins is in a packet, let's say. The number of trades between neighbors is equal to the number of lines connecting the circles, which means 30 trades. Two packets of coins are delivered along each line, one from each student to the other, so 60 packets of coins are traded. 3360/60=56, so each packet has 56 coins. The number of packets a student has is equal to the number of neighbors that student has. The middle student has 5 neighbors, so has 5 packets, therefore 5*56=280 coins.

Since we can deduce that all the coins are weighted equally by seeing that all the students will have the same number of coins after trading, we get, $4x(5)+4x(5)+3x(5)+5x=3360$ Simplifying, $60x=3360$ $x=56$ We want the middle circle, $56(5)=\boxed{280}$

"Since we can deduce that all the coins are weighted equally by seeing that all the students will have the same number of coins after trading" ... but where's the proof? :| http://i.imgur.com/GaROCFh.png Consider the green ring. let this person have $5x$ coins. since this person spreads his coins equally to the orange ring, he gives them each $x$. this means that each person in the orange ring will also give the green ring exactly $x$ coins since each person gets the same coins they started with by the end of it all (given). now consider the orange , we already know that each of them spread their coins in increments of $x$ (coins are spread evenly (given) so if they each give green ring $x$ coins, they must also give the people in the blue ring $x$ coins each). this means each person in the orange has $3\cdot{x}=3x$ coins each since they each have a network of 3 people. now consider the blue ring. since each person in orange has $3x$ coins, the people in blue must give each person in orange exactly $x$ coins to keep the fact that the people in orange have to give each person in blue $x$ coins (notice the giving of coins is reciprocal by definition). thus the people in blue must also spread their coins in increments of $x$, and since there are 4 people in each blue-ring network, they have $4x$ coins each. similarly the people in the pink ring have $4x$ coins each. this means there are $60x$ coins overall $\implies$ $60x=3360\Leftrightarrow{x=56}$ so green ring has exactly $\boxed{280}$ coins.

You can also prove by induction that the amount given to each of your neighbors is the same for all students. This is equivalent to proving that the 'net transaction' along each edge of the graph is 0. The base case is the center; by symmetry the transaction amount along each of the 5 adjacent edges is equal; they have to sum to 0, so each must be 0. Next the transactions adjacent to each person in the first ring is 2 equal transactions by symmetry, plus another which we already know to be 0. The sum of those 3 transactions also has to be 0, so the 2 equal ones were 0. Identical logic as we continue going outward.

Does this logic make sense? Let $x$= number of circles one circle is connected to. Then we get $5x+15x+20x+20x=3360$, and thus $5x=280$

Let the center have $5x$ coins. Then based on the amount of neighbors to each person, we can create the equation $5x + 5 \cdot 3x + 5 \cdot 4x + 5 \cdot 4x = 3360$, so $60x = 3360 \implies x = 56$. The answer is $5 \cdot 56 = \boxed{280}$.

Solution: Let the center initially have $n$ coins. Then, after the center circle gives away all its coins, it gains all $n$ back through its five direct neighbors. (Call the direct neighbors of the center circle semi-center .) Hence, all semi-center circles give $\frac{n}{5}$ coins to its neighbors. Since those circles have three neighbors, they start out with $\frac{3n}{5}$ coins. (Call the direct neighbors of the semi-center circles semi-outer .) Notice that, since one of the semi-center circles starts out, and therefore, must end with $\frac{3n}{5}$ coins, and the center circle gives $\frac{n}{5}$ coins, each semi-outer circle gives $\frac{n}{5}$ to each of its two semi-center neighbors, and, therefore, gives that amount to the two of its mostouter neighbor circles as well. Hence, the semi-outer circles start with $\frac{4n}{5}$ coins. Furthermore, this means that the outermost circles give $\frac{n}{5}$ coins to the semi-outer circles (since the semi-center circles give that many coins to their neighbors). Since the outermost circles have four neighbors, they start out with $\frac{4n}{5}$ coins. In total, all the circles start out with $n + 5\frac{3n}{5} + 5\frac{4n}{5} + 5\frac{4n}{5} = 12n$ coins. Finally, we get that $12n = 3360 \Rightarrow n = \boxed{280}$.

I had a solution using Dijkstra's or Bellman Ford's , but it's a long solution...

Say the center has $5k$ coins. Then each of the students he gives coins to recieves $k$ coins from him. Hence all students in the second layer from the center have $3k$ coins. Then all students in the third layer from the center have $4k$ coins. Finally all students in the outermost layer have $4k$ coins as well. Then we have a total of $5k + 15k + 20k + 20k = 3360 \implies k = 56$. Then the middle student has $280$ coins.

title: Logarithms in x,y,z
problem_text: Let $x$, $y$, and $z$ be positive real numbers that satisfy \ The value of $xy^5z$ can be expressed in the form $\frac{1}{2^{p/q}}$, where $p$ and $q$ are relatively prime integers. Find $p+q$.
solution_text:
Let $\log_x2y=a$. Since $2\log_x2y=2\log_{2x}4z=\log_{2x^4}8yz$, we have \ Multiplying the first two equations together we obtain \ Which simplifies to $x^{6a}=\frac{1}{2^a}$. Similarly, $z=\frac{1}{2^{\frac{-5a+12}{6}}}$. Thus, we have the value of $xy^5z$ equal to \ We obtain the answer of $43+6=049$.

p7: Let each of the quantities be equal to $r$. Then $x^{\frac{r}{2}} = 2y, 2^{\frac{r}{2}}x^{\frac{r}{2}} = 4z, 2^r x^{4r} = 8yz$. Multiplying the first two equations and equating to the third gives $2^{\frac{r}{2}} \cdot x^r = 2^{r} \cdot x^{4r}$, or $2^{\frac{r}{2}} \cdot x^{3r} = 1$. Squaring, we get $2^{r} \cdot x^{6r} = 1$, or $x^{6r} = \frac{1}{2^r}$, which gives $x = \frac{1}{2^{\frac{1}{6}}}$. Next, $(2y)^5 = 32y^5 = x^{\frac{5r}{2}} = \frac{1}{2^{\frac{5r}{12}}}$, so $y^5 = \frac{1}{2^{\frac{5r+60}{12}}}$. Lastly, $x^{\frac{r}{2}} = \frac{1}{2^{\frac{r}{12}}}$, so $4z = 2^{\frac{5r}{12}}$, or $z = 2^{\frac{5r-24}{12}}$. These three have product $2^{-7} \cdot 2^{-\frac{1}{6}} = 2^{-\frac{43}{6}}$ after simplification, giving an answer of $\boxed{\frac{43}{6}}$.

Let $a=\log_2x$, $b=\log_2y$, $c=\log_2z$. With $\log/\exp$ rules we rewrite the equations as: $$\frac{2b+2}a=\frac{2c+4}{a+1}=\frac{b+c+3}{4a+1}$$ and we want to find $a+5b+c=-\frac pq$. Recall that $\frac pq=\frac rs=t$ implies $t=\frac{p+r}{q+s}$, so applying this here gives: $$\frac{2b+2c+6}{2a+1}=\frac{b+c+3}{4a+1}\Rightarrow a=-\frac16.$$ Then the equations rewrite as: $$-\frac13b-\frac13=\frac{12}5c+\frac{24}5=3b+3c+9$$ or $$-5b-5=36c+72=45b+45c+135.$$ The second equality implies $45b+45c+135=36c+72$, or $45b+9c+63=0$, or $5b+c+7=0$. Then $a+5b+c=-\frac16-7=-\frac{43}6$, so the answer is $43+6=\boxed{049}$.

We are given $$2\log_x(2y) = 2\log_{2x}(4z) = \log_{2x^4}(8yz) = 2a$$ Then, \begin{align*} x^a &= 2y\\ 2^{a} \cdot x^{a} &= 4z\\ 2^{2a} \cdot x^{8a} &= 8yz \end{align*} Subbing the first into the second we have $2^a = \frac{2z}{y}$. Then squaring and subbing into the third we have $\frac{4z^2}{y^2} \cdot x^{8a} = 8yz \implies x^{8a} = \frac{2y^3}{z}$. Raising the first to the eighth power and subbing in we have $256y^8 = \frac{2y^3}{z}$ and hence $y^5z = \frac{1}{2^7}$. Then it suffices to find $x$. Multiplying the first two equations and equating to the third we have $x^{2a} \cdot 2^{a} = 2^{2a} \cdot x^{8a} \implies x^2 \cdot 2 = 2^2 \cdot x^8 \implies x = \frac{1}{2^{\frac{1}{6}}}$. Then our final answer is $\frac{43}{6}$.

So, $xy^5z=x=2^{-1/6} \cdot 2^{\frac{-65}{12}} \cdot 2^{\frac{-19}{12}}=2^\frac{-43}{6}$ and we get $p+q = 43 + 6 = \boxed{49.}$

Let the common value be $k$. Then, \begin{align*} x^k &= 4y^2\\ (2x)^k &= 16z^2\\ (2x^4)^k &= 8yz \end{align*} Multiplying the first and second and dividing by the square of the last we have, \begin{align*} \frac{x^k \cdot (2x)^k}{(2x^4)^{2k}} &= 1\\ \iff x^k \cdot (2x)^k &= (2x^4)^{2k}\\ \iff x \cdot (2x) &= (2x^4)^2 \end{align*} which yields $2x^2 = 4x^8$ or $x= \frac{1}{2^{\frac{1}{6}}}$. Now we have, \begin{align*} 2^{-\frac{k}{12} - 1} &= y\\ 2^{\frac{k}{2} - \frac{k}{12} - 2} &= z \end{align*} Then $xy^5z = 2^{-\frac{43}{6}}$ which yields an answer of $\boxed{049}$.

First, we can move the $2$'s inside the logarithms by squaring the stuff inside: $\log_{x}(4y^2) = \log_{2x}(16z^2) = \log_{2x^4}(8yz)$. Setting these 3 expressions to equal $a$, we can get rid of the logarithms by using $\log_{a}b = c \implies a^c = b$. The resulting equations are: \begin{align*} x^a &= 4y^2 & (1) \\ 2^a x^a &= 16z^2 & (2) \\ 2^a x^{4a} &= 8yz & (3) \\ \end{align*} Multiplying equations $(1)$ and $(2)$ yields $$2^a x^{2a} = 64y^2z^2$$ Squaring equation $(3)$ yields $$2^{2a} x^{8a} = 64y^2z^2$$ Equating yields \begin{align*} 2^a x^{2a} &= 2^{2a} x^{8a} \\ 2^{-a} &= x^{6a} \\ x &= 2^{-\frac{1}{6}} \\ \end{align*} Dividing equation $(3)$ by equation $(2)$ yields $$x^{3a} = 2^{-1}yz^{-1}$$ Cubing equation $(1)$ yields $$x^{3a} = 2^6y^6$$ Equating yields \begin{align*} 2^{-1}yz^{-1} &= 2^6y^6 \\ z^{-1} &= 2^7y^5\\ z &= 2^{-7}y^{-5}\\ \end{align*} Thus, $xy^5z = 2^{-\frac{1}{6}} y^5 2^{-7}y^{-5} = 2^{-\frac{43}{6}} = \dfrac{1}{2^{\frac{43}{6}}} \implies p+q = \boxed{49}$.

author: AoPS
title: Sets of perfect squares ending in 256
problem_text: Let $\mathcal{S}$ be the set of all perfect squares whose rightmost three digits in base $10$ are $256$. Let $\mathcal{T}$ be the set of all numbers of the form $\frac{x-256}{1000}$, where $x$ is in $\mathcal{S}$. In other words, $\mathcal{T}$ is the set of numbers that result when the last three digits of each number in $\mathcal{S}$ are truncated. Find the remainder when the tenth smallest element of $\mathcal{T}$ is divided by $1000$.
solution_text:
If the last three digits of the square are 256, the number is congruent to +-16 mod 500. The tenth smallest number is 2500-16, so squaring, subtracting 256, and dividing by 1000, we get 6170. We need to find the remainder when 6170 is divided by 1000, so it is 170.

Let $\mathcal{S}$ be the set of all perfect squares whose rightmost three digits in base $10$ are $256$. Let $\mathcal{T}$ be the set of all numbers of the form $\frac{x-256}{1000}$, where $x$ is in $\mathcal{S}$. In other words, $\mathcal{T}$ is the set of numbers that result when the last three digits of each number in $\mathcal{S}$ are truncated. Find the remainder when the tenth smallest element of $\mathcal{T}$ is divided by $1000$. yes i also got 170

We don't really need to think about the set $\mathcal{T}$; it is only used in the problem statement to make the actual question being asked easier to state. All numbers $x^2$ in $\mathcal{S}$ satisfy $x^2 - 16^2 \equiv 0 \pmod {1000}$, so $(x + 16)(x - 16) \equiv 0 \pmod {1000}$. Obviously, $x \equiv \pm 16 \pmod {1000}$ works. Are there any other solutions? Somehow we have to fit all the factors of 1000 into $(x+16) * (x-16)$, which includes three 5s and three 2s. Since $x + 16$ and $x - 16$ differ by 32, obviously all three factors of 5 must be in one of them. Also, if that number is not divisible by 4, then the second one will not be either, and not enough factors of two will be present; so one of $x \pm 16$ must have three factors of 5 and two factors of 2. Thus, we see all solutions are of the form $x \equiv 0 \pm 16$ or $x \equiv 500 \pm 16 \pmod {1000}$. Listing out possible values of $x$: $16, 500 \pm 16, 1000 \pm 16, 1500 \pm 16, 2000 \pm 16, 2500 \pm 16$... With $x = 2500 - 16$ being the 10th solution. $(2500-16)^2 = 2500^2 - 180000 + 256 = 6250000 - 180000 + 256 = 6170256$, and so the answer is $\boxed{170}$.

Basically, the perfect squares are in form $(10a \pm 16)^2$, with $a$ being a nonnegative integer. So squaring gives $1000|100a^2 + 320a$, so $50|a(5a + 32)$ and $a$ is a multiple of 50. The 10th smallest number is 2484, so squaring gives 6170256, and the answer is $\boxed{170}$. This was really similar to a WOOT practice AIME problem..

So I realized the number ended in ab4 or ab6, where a and b are digits; putting a 1, 2, etc. in the thousands place doesn't do anything mod 1000. (100a+10b+4)(100a+10b+4) Looking at the relevant places (read: hundreds down to ones) we see 800a+100b^2+80b+16. Note that the 80b is the only thing impacting the tens digit; 1+(80b mod 10)=5. Thus, b=3 or 8. Plugging this in, we see that for b=3, we have 800a+1156, which is impossible if the hundreds digit is 2; for b=8, we have 800a+7056, and thus a=4 or 9. Performing similar calculations on (100a+10b+6)(100a+10b+6) we find that b=1 or 6, but b=6 doesn't work. For b=1, a=0 or 5. Thus, our solutions end in 016,284,516,or 984. We can see that the 10th smallest solution will be 2284, and then we square to get 6170256, leaving a solution of 6170 (mod 1000) = 170.

A way to obtain $500k\pm 16$ as the square roots of the elements of $S$ is to consider $(a+16)(a-16)\equiv 0\pmod{1000}$ as equivalent to $(a+16)(a-16)\equiv 0\pmod 8$ and $(a+16)(a-16)\equiv 0\pmod {125}$. The latter condition holds iff 125 divides either $a+16$ or $a-16$, while the former condition holds iff 4 divides either $a+16$ or $a-16$. (2 is not enough, while 4 is sufficient since the other factor will be even.) In fact this means that 4 must and will divide both. So this means that 500 divides either $a+16$ or $a-16$. Then we proceed as normal.

author: AoPS
title: Froggy
problem_text: A frog begins at $P_0 = (0,0)$ and makes a sequence of jumps according to the following rule: from $P_n=(x_n,y_n)$, the frog jumps to $P_{n+1}$, which may be any of the points $(x_n+7, y_n+2)$, $(x_n+2,y_n+7)$, $(x_n-5, y_n-10)$, or $(x_n-10,y_n-5)$. There are $M$ points $(x,y)$ with $|x|+|y| \le 100$ that can be reached by a sequence of such jumps. Find the remainder when $M$ is divided by $1000$.
solution_text:
I realized that $y-x$ is invariant mod $5$ and $y+x$ is invariant mod $3$, and that all values that preserved that were possible. Then you draw a grid and multiply the number of lines going way with those going the other way. I ended up with $577$, which I believe is wrong...

Assuming that $3|x+y$ and $5|x-y$ is sufficient-- this shouldn't be hard to show-- I have checked on Python that $M=1373$. Note that $x+y\equiv 0 \pmod 3, x-y \equiv 0 \pmod 5 \iff x\equiv 11y \pmod{15}$. >>> m=0 >>> for x in range(-100,100): ... for y in range(-100+abs(x),100-abs(x)+1): ... m += int((x+4*y)%15==0) >>> m 1373 I'm not sure how one would count the number of points by hand though; this is where I got stuck on the actual test.

I did it by considering the number of such points on each of the lines x+y=k for -100<=k<=100. Obviously, only 3|k works, and it can be verified that they form a pattern of 20 and 21 points for each k divisible by 3.

I also noted that $x+y$ is always 0 mod 3 and $x=y$ mod 5, and I was able to prove that any point satisfying those 2 conditions worked, but I couldn't count the number of them on the test :(

Here is my work for the problem: This is basically the same idea as yugrey's. Since 3|(x+y), we can write x + y = 3a, for some integer a. We find that -33 <= a <= 33 in order for the line to pass through the region in interest. There are 67 such lines (since 67 possible values of a). Similarly, since 5|(x-y), we can write x - y = 5b, for some integer b. The bounds on b are -20 <= b <= 20. There are 41 such lines. Each point attainable is determined by the intersection of a line of slope 1 and a line of slope -1. So there are (67 * 41) such interesections, producing 2747 points. So I got 747 as my answer.

Each point attainable is determined by the intersection of a line of slope 1 and a line of slope -1. So there are (67 * 41) such interesections, producing 2747 points. Yes, but... $x$ and $y$ must both integers, so $x+y$ and $x-y$ must have the same parity. Thus, if $x+y=3a$ and $x-y=5b$ for some $a,b$, then $a$ and $b$ must have the same parity. $-33\le a\le 33$ and $-20\le b\le 20$, so there are 34 odd and 33 even values of $a$, while there are 20 odd and 21 even values of $b$, so the number of attainable points is $(34)(20)+(33)(21)=1373$, making our answer $\boxed{373}$.

Unfortunately, I didn't think of a good way to count them given the rather contrived boundaries, until about 5 minutes before the end: The 15x15 squares completely contained within the desired region each have 15 solutions. For the other 15x15 squares partly contained (those where the least extreme corner has |x|+|y|=90), we need the absolute values of the extra bits of x and y to add to at most 10. Unfortunately, I didn't have time to calculate this info for all 15 solutions for all 4 quadrants. Oh well.

Note the conditions $3|x+y$ and $5|x-y$. Then, $x,y$ can be written as $x=\frac{3q-5p}{2}$, $y=\frac{3q+5p}{2}$ where $p,q$ are integers of the same parity. Now, we just need to find the number of solutions to $|3p-5q|+|3q+5p|\leq{200}$ where $p,q$ are of the same parity. But this is a simply a square with vertices $(33,20), (33,-20),(-33,-20),(-33,20)$. The number of coordinate pairs $(p,q)$ in this square with $p-q$ even is $1$ less than those with $p-q$ odd. Since the total number of points is $(41)(67)=2746$, $M=1373$ and the answer is $373$.

author: AoPS
title: Steiner's Theorem Returns
problem_text: Let $\triangle ABC$ be a right triangle with right angle at $C$. Let $D$ and $E$ be points on $\overline{AB}$ with $D$ between $A$ and $E$ such that $\overline{CD}$ and $\overline{CE}$ trisect $\angle C$. If $\frac{DE}{BE} = \frac{8}{15}$, then $\tan B$ can be written as $\frac{m\sqrt{p}}{n}$, where $m$ and $n$ are relatively prime positive integers, and $p$ is a positive integer not divisible by the square of any prime. Find $m+n+p$.
solution_text:
Using the Law of Sines on triangles BEC and DEC gives: EC = 30cosA= 16sin(30+A). From here we can find tanA and note that 1/tanA=tanB.

Because we are asked to find the tangent, we are dealing with ratios, so we can assume that $DE=8, BE=15$. By the Angle Bisector Theorem, we let $CD=8x, CB=15x$. Applying the Law of Cosines on $\angle DAB$ gives $23^2=64x^2+225x^2-120x^2$, so $x=\frac{23}{13}$. Then apply the Law of Sines to the same triangle to get $\frac{CD}{sin B}=\frac{23}{sin 60^\circ}$. Since CD=$8x=\frac{184}{13}$, we easily find that $sin B=\frac{4\sqrt{3}}{13}$, so $cos B=\frac{11}{13}$, so the tangent is $\boxed{\frac{4\sqrt{3}}{11}}$, so m+n+p=18.

$CE$ bisects $\angle DCB$, so by the angle bisector theorem $\frac{CD}{CB} = \frac{DE}{EB} = \frac{8}{15}$. By the law of sines on $\triangle CDB$, we have $\frac{CD}{CB} = \frac{\sin \angle CBD}{\sin \angle CDB} = \frac{\sin B}{\sin (120^{\circ} - B)}.$ Thus, \ Consequently, \ so our answer is $4+3+11 = \boxed{018}$.

Let $DE=8x$, $BE=15x$, and $CE=y$. By Law of Sines on CBE, we get $\frac{\sin{30}}{15x}=\frac{\sin B}{y} \implies \frac{1}{2}y=15x\sin B$ By Law of Sines on CDE, we get $\frac{\sin 30}{8x}=\frac{\sin(120-B)}{y}$, since $\angle CDB=180-(60)-(B)=120-B$. This gives $\frac{1}{2}y=8x\sin(120-B)$ Equating the two, we have $15x\sin B=8x\sin(120-B) \implies 15\sinh=8\sin(120-B)$. Since $\sin(x)=\sin(180-x)$, we have $15\sinh=8\sin(B+60) \implies 15\sin B=8(\sinh\cos60+\sin60\cosh)=8(\frac{1}{2}\sinh+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot \cos B)$ Thus, $15\sin B=4\sin B+4\cos B\sqrt{3} \implies 11\sin B=4\cos B\sqrt{3} \implies \frac{\sin B}{\cos B}=\frac{4\sqrt{3}}{11}$. The answer is thus $4+3+11=018$.

author: AoPS
title: Cocentric Circles of radii 3,4,5
problem_text: Three concentric circles have radii $3$, $4$, and $5$. An equilateral triangle with one vertex on each circle has side length $s$. The largest possible area of the triangle can be written as $a+\frac{b}{c}\sqrt{d}$, where $a,b,c$ and $d$ are positive integers, $b$ and $c$ are relatively prime, and $d$ is not divisible by the square of any prime. Find $a+b+c+d$.
solution_text:
Let $O$ be the center of the circles, and let the vertices of the triangle be $A$, $B$, and $C$, such that $OA = 3$, $OB = 4$, and $OC = 5$. Let the image of $O$ upon reflection across $\overline{AB}$ be $O_c$, and define $O_b$ and $O_a$ similarly. Note that $AO_b = AO_c = 3$, $BO_a = BO_c = 4$, and $CO_a = CO_b = 5$. Furthermore, $\angle O_cAO_b = \angle O_aBO_c = \angle O_aCO_b = 120^\circ$. Triangle $O_aO_bO_c$ has the side lengths $3\sqrt{3}$, $4\sqrt{3}$, $5\sqrt{3}$, so it is a right triangle. Now, \begin{align*} & = + + + \\ & = \frac{1}{2} 3^2 \sin 120^\circ + \frac{1}{2} 4^2 \sin 120^\circ + \frac{1}{2} 5^2 \sin 120^\circ + \frac{1}{2} \cdot 3\sqrt{3} \cdot 4\sqrt{3} \\ &= \frac{25\sqrt{3}}{2} + 18 \end{align*} The area of hexagon $AO_bCO_aBO_c$ is twice the area of $ABC$, so $ = 9 + \frac{25}{4} \sqrt{3}$ and $a + b + c + d = \boxed{041}$.

Assume $a=3,$ then we have $|b|=4,|c|=5,$ and by EGMO Lemma 6.28 our triangle is equilateral iff $3^2+b^2+c^2=3(b+c)+bc.$ This solves using the quadratic formula as $$b^2-b(c+3)+c^2-3c+9=0$$ $$2b=3+c \pm \sqrt{(c+3)-4(c^2-3c+9)}=3+c \pm i\sqrt{3}(c-3).$$ Assume it's the positive case , and solving for $c$ yields $c=\frac{2b-3+3i\sqrt{3}}{1+i\sqrt{3}}.$ Taking the magnitude of both sides, $$5=|c|=\left|\frac{2b-3+3i\sqrt{3}}{1+i\sqrt{3}}\right|=\frac{|2b-3+3i\sqrt{3}|}{|1+i\sqrt{3}|}$$ thus $10=|2b-3+3i\sqrt{3}|.$ Let $b=p+qi$ for reals $p,q,$ then $|b|=4$ is equivalent to $$p^2+q^2=16\implies q=-\sqrt{16-p^2}$$ where we assume the negative case to maximize area (we set $a=3$). We also derive $$10=|2p-3+i(2q+3\sqrt{3})|$$ which yields $$(2p-3)^2+(2q+3\sqrt{3})^2=100.$$ Substituting, $$(2p-3)^2+(2(-\sqrt{16-p^2})+3\sqrt{3})^2=100$$ $$4p^2-12p-91=-(2\sqrt{16-p^2}-3\sqrt{3})^2$$ $$4p^2-12p-91=-4(16-p^2)+12\sqrt{3}\sqrt{16-p^2}-27$$ And somehow, magically, this cancels to $$\cancel{4p^2}-12p\cancel{-91}=\cancel{-64}+\cancel{4p^2}+12\sqrt{48-3p^2}\cancel{-27}$$ $$-p=\sqrt{48-3p^2} \implies p=-2\sqrt{3}.$$ The area of our triangle can be expressed as $\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{4}|b-3|^2,$ and note that $$|b-3|^2=(b-3)(\overline{b}-3)=b\overline{b}-3(b+\overline{b})+9,$$ and since $16=|b|^2=b\overline{b},$ our answer is equivalent to $$\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}(25-3(b+\overline{b}))=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}(25-6p)=\boxed{9+\frac{25\sqrt{3}}{4}}.$$

Clearly, in order to maximize the area of the triangle, we want the center of the three circles to lie inside of the triangle, so we can rephrase the problem in the following way: Given an equilateral triangle $\triangle{ABC}$ with a point $P$ inside of it satisfying $$PA=3, PB=4, PC=5,$$ compute the area of the triangle. \\ Let us rotate $\triangle{APB}$ by $60^\circ$ about $A$ so that the image of $P$ is a point $P'$ on the opposite side of $\overline{AC}$ as $B.$ Then, we easily see that $\triangle{PAP'}$ is equilateral, so $PP'=3.$ Then, we also have $CP=5$ and $CP'=4,$ so it follows that $\triangle{PP'C}$ is a 3-4-5 triangle. This tells us that \begin{align*} \angle{AP'C} &= \angle{AP'P}+\angle{PP'C} \\ &= 60^\circ + 90^\circ \\ &= 150^\circ, \end{align*} so the Law of Cosines gives us $$AC^2 = 25 + 12\sqrt{3}.$$ Hence, the area of $\triangle{ABC}$ is equal to $$\frac{AC^2 \sqrt{3}}{4} = 9 + \tfrac{25}{4}\sqrt{3},$$ so the answer is $9+25+4+3=\boxed{41}.$ $\square$ \\

author: AoPS
title: The AIME Triathlon
problem_text: The AIME Triathlon consists of a half-mile swim, a $30$-mile bicycle, and an eight-mile run. Tom swims, bicycles, and runs at constant rates. He runs five times as fast as he swims, and he bicycles twice as fast as he runs. Tom completes the AIME Triathlon in four and a quarter hours. How many minutes does he spend bicycling?
solution_text:
Let $s$ be the rate in miles per hour at which Tom swims. Then his running speed is $5s$ and his bicycling speed is $10s$. We know $d=rt$, so $t=\frac{d}{r}$. Therefore we can set the sum of $\frac{d}{r}$ for each segment of the triatholon equal to the total time, $\frac{17}{4}$ hours. This yields the equation $\frac{\frac{1}{2}}{s}+\frac{30}{10s}+\frac{8}{5s}=\frac{17}{4}$. Solving gives us $s=\frac{6}{5}$. The time Tom spends bicycling is then simply $\frac{30}{10s}=\frac{3}{s}=\frac{3}{\frac{6}{5}}=\frac{5}{2}$ hours, or $\boxed{150}$ minutes.

Let $b$ be Tom's hourly cycling rate, $r$ be his hourly running rate, and $s$ be his hourly swimming rate all in miles. We are given $r = 5s$ and $b = 2r$ which is equivalent to $10s = 2r = b$. Because $d = rt$, $\frac{1}{2} = st_{s}$, $30 = bt_{b}$, and $8 = rt_{r}$ where $t_{s}, t_{b},$ and $t_{r}$ are his times in the respective events. We get $t_{s} = \frac{1}{2s}$, $t_{b} = \frac{30}{b}$, and $t_{r} = \frac{8}{r}$. Tom completes the triathlon in $\frac{17}{4}$ hours so we have $\frac{17}{4} = \frac{1}{2s} + \frac{30}{b} + \frac{8}{r}$. From $10s = 2r = b$, we write $r$ and $s$ in terms of $b$. We get $\frac{1}{2s} = \frac{1}{\frac{b}{5}} = \frac{5}{b}$, $\frac{30}{b} = \frac{30}{b}$, and $\frac{8}{r} = \frac{8}{\frac{b}{2}} = \frac{16}{b}$. Hence we have $\frac{17}{4} = \frac{5}{b} + \frac{30}{b} + \frac{16}{b} = \frac{51}{b}$. We get $b = 12$ so Tom's cycling rate is 12 miles an hour. He bikes a total of 30 miles so his time in hours is $\frac{30}{12}$. We are asked for minutes so we multiply by 60 and get $\boxed{150}$ minutes.

Let $x$ be the time it takes for Tom to do his bike ride, clearly his time per mile is $\frac{x}{30}$. Since he runs half as fast as he bicycles, and there is inverse proportion between the speed and the amount of minutes, it takes him $\frac{x}{30} \cdot 2 \rightarrow \frac{x}{15}$ minutes to the mile when running, and it therefore takes him $\frac{x}{15} \cdot 5 \rightarrow \frac{x}{3}$ minutes to the mile when swimming (because we again notice the inverse proportion). Since he runs $8$ miles, it takes him $8 \cdot \frac{x}{15} \rightarrow \frac{8x}{15}$ minutes running, and it takes $\frac{x}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rightarrow \frac{x}{6}$ minutes swimming. Therefore $x + \frac{8x}{15} + \frac{x}{6} = 255 \rightarrow \frac{102x}{60} = 255$. Therefore $102x = 60 \cdot 255 \rightarrow \boxed{150}$.

We let $x$ be the number of minutes Tom spends bicycling. We then know that $\frac{x}{6}+x+\frac{8x}{15}=255$. We get that $\frac{17x}{10}=255$ so $x=\frac{10}{17}255=\boxed{150}$.

Let's say he swims at $x$ miles per hour. Then he runs at $5x$ miles per hour and bicycles at $10x$ miles per hour. This gives the equation $\frac{\frac{1}{2}}{x}+\frac{30}{10x}+\frac{8}{5x}=4.25$. This means $x=1.2$, so he bicycles at $12$ miles per hour. This means it takes him $\frac{30}{12} \cdot 60 = \boxed{150}$ minutes to finish the bicycling section.

Let $s$ be Tom's speed while swimming. Then, it's easy to derive \ and so the speed when Tom bikes is $12$ miles per hour. This means $\tfrac{30}{12}\cdot 60=\boxed{150}$ minutes spent biking.

The first digit must be $5,$ also the same for the last. It can't be $0$, because the first digit can't be $0.$ Hence, this number is in the form of $5abc5.$ Now, pick $2$ of the $3$ digits. Then there exist $2$, options for the last digit. Hence, $10^2\cdot 2=\boxed{200}.$ fally latexed solution

Lemma: If we choose to arbitrary one-digit numbers $x, y,$ there is exactly two one-digit numbers $z$ such that $x+y+z \equiv 0 \pmod{5}.$ Proof: We must have that $5n \leq x+y \leq 5(n+1),$ so there will be exactly one $z$ such that $x+y+z = 5(n+1)$ and one $z$ such that $x+y+z = 5(n+2).$ Since $5(n+3) - x - y \geq 10,$ we therefore have exactly two one-digit values of $z$ such that $x+y+z \equiv 0 \pmod{5}$ for any arbitrary one-digit number $x, y.$ Solution: The last digit of the five-digit number may be $0,$ or $5.$ But the first digit cannot be $0,$ so the number must be in the form $\overline{5xyz5}.$ Now if we want that $5+x+y+z+5 \equiv 0 \pmod{5},$ we must have $x+y+z \equiv 0 \pmod{5}.$ Using the lemma above, we find that we can choose the $x, y$ in $10^2$ ways, and the $z$ in $2$ ways. Therefore our final answer would equal $10^2 \cdot 2 = 2\cdot 100 = \boxed{200}.$

author: AoPS
title: Two Squares and Two Rectangles
problem_text: Let $ABCD$ be a square, and let $E$ and $F$ be points on $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{BC}$, respectively. The line through $E$ parallel to $\overline{BC}$ and the line through $F$ parallel to $\overline{AB}$ divide $ABCD$ into two squares and two non square rectangles. The sum of the areas of the two squares is $\frac{9}{10}$ of the area of square $ABCD$. Find $\frac{AE}{EB} + \frac{EB}{AE}$.
solution_text:
WLOG, let ABCD be a unit square. Let $AE=x$ (side length of small square) then $EB =1-x$ (side length of large square). Since the total area is 1 then the sum of the two squares is $x^2+(1-x)^2=\frac{9}{10}$. We want to find $\frac{x}{1-x}+\frac{1-x}{x}=\frac{x^2+(1-x)^2}{x(1-x)}$. We already calculated the numerator, $9/10$. Notice that $(x+(1-x))^2=1^2=x^2+(1-x)^2+2x(1-x)$ which we can simplify to $2x(1-x)=1-(x^2+(1-x)^2)=1-\frac{9}{10}=\frac{1}{10}$ so $x(1-x)=\frac{1}{20}$. Thus $\frac{x^2+(1-x)^2}{x(1-x)}=\frac{9/10}{1/20}=\boxed{018}$

Let $AE=a, EB=b$. Then we have: $a^2+b^2=\frac{9}{10}\cdot(a+b)^2$ Expand and cancel out: $a^2+b^2=\frac{9}{10}\cdot(a+b)^2$ $\frac{1}{10}a^2+\frac{1}{10}b^2=\frac{9}{10}\cdot2ab$ Therefore, $a^2+b^2=18ab$ Divide both side by $ab$ $\frac{a}{b}+\frac{b}{a}=\boxed{018}$

WLOG let the side length of the square be $10$. Since two squares are formed by the lines we must have $BE=BF$. Let $a=BE=BF$ and $b=AE=FC$ so that $a+b=10$ and $a^2+b^2=\dfrac9{10}(100)=90$. Then $a^2+2ab+b^2=100$, so $ab=5$ and $\dfrac ab+\dfrac ba=\dfrac {a^2+b^2}{ab}=\dfrac{90}5=\boxed{018}$.

Let one of them be $a$ and the other be $b$. We are given $\frac{a^2 + b^2}{a^2 + 2ab + b^2} = \frac{9}{10}$ so $\frac{a^2 + b^2}{2ab} = \frac{9}{10 - 9}$. $\frac{a}{b} + \frac{b}{a} = \frac{a^2 + b^2}{ab} = \boxed{018}$.

Was I the only one who let the side be one and do the 35-second bash with the root 5's. Ugh. ;) WLOG have the side length of the square be $10$. In order for their to be two squares that account for $9/10$ of the area of the square with side length $10$, we draw the lines going through $\bold{E}$ and $\bold{F}$ such that a large square and a small square are formed touching at a single point along the diagonal $BD$ (or $AC$) of the square. Two rectangles are also formed. We let the side length of the larger internal square be $x$ and the length of the smaller internal square be $y$. The area of the square is $10^2=100$. Therefore, $x^2+y^2=9/10\cdot100=90$. The sum of $x$ and $y$ is $10$ as the side lengths of the internal squares add up to the length of a side of the original square. We have $(x+y)^2=x^2+2xy+y^2=100$. $2xy=10$ $xy=5$ With Vieta'a formulas, we form a quadratic with the $x$ term equal to the negative of the sum of the side lengths of the internal squares and the constant term equal to the product of the side lenths of the internal squares. Visually, this gives us: $x^2-10x+5=0$ The roots are $5\pm2\sqrt{5}$. These are the two side lengths of the internal squares, and are also the lengths of $AE$ and $EB$. Thus we have: $\frac{5+2\sqrt{5}}{5-2\sqrt{5}}+\frac{5-2\sqrt{5}}{5+2\sqrt{5}}$ $=\frac{25+20+25+20}{25-20}$ $=\frac{90}{5}$ $=\boxed{018}$

pair A = (0, 0); pair B = (2, 0); pair C = (2, -2); pair D = (0, -2); draw(A--B--C--D--cycle); dot("$A$", A, dir(180)); dot("$B$", B); dot("$C$", C); dot("$D$", D, dir(180)); pair E = B - (0.5, 0); pair F = A - (0, 0.5); pair G = foot(F, B, C); pair H = foot(E, D, C); dot("$F$", F, dir(180)); dot("$E$", E, dir(90)); draw(E -- H); draw(F -- G); Let $AE = x$ and $BE = y$, therefore the area of the squares are $x^2$ and $y^2$, and the area of the two rectangles are both $xy$. So we have $x^2 + y^2 = \frac{9}{10}(x^2 + y^2 + 2xy)$, multiplying both sides by $\frac{10}{9}$, we have $\frac{10}{9}x^2 + \frac{10}{9}y^2 = x^2 + y^2 + 2xy \rightarrow \frac{x^2 + y^2}{9} = 2xy \rightarrow x^2 + y^2 = 18xy$. We know that $\frac{x}{y} + \frac{y}{x} \rightarrow \frac{x^2}{xy} + \frac{y^2}{xy} \rightarrow \frac{x^2 + y^2}{xy}$, meaning that we just have to divide both sides by $xy$, which leaves the RHS with $\boxed{18}$.

Let $AE=x$ and $EB=y-x$. Then we have $x^2+(y-x)^2=\frac{9}{10}y^2$, meaning that $20x^2+y^2=20xy$. We want $\frac{x}{y-x}+\frac{y-x}{x}$. Let's focus on finding $\frac{y-x}{x}=\frac{y}{x}-1$. Dividing both sides of $20x^2+y^2=20xy$ by $xy$, we get $20\frac{x}{y}+\frac{y}{x}=20$. Let $\frac{y}{x}=a$. Then we have $\frac{20}{a}+a=20$, or $a=10+4\sqrt5$. Subtracting one gives $\frac{y-x}{x}=9+4\sqrt5$. Taking the reciprocal gives $\frac{x}{y-x}=9-4\sqrt5$, and adding gives $\frac{x}{y-x}+\frac{y-x}{x}=\boxed{18}$.

author: AoPS
title: Not Burnside's Lemma
problem_text: In the array of $13$ squares shown below, $8$ squares are colored red, and the remaining $5$ squares are colored blue. If one of all possible such colorings is chosen at random, the probability that the chosen colored array appears the same when rotated $90^{\circ}$ around the central square is $\tfrac{1}{n}$, where $n$ is a positive integer. Find $n$. draw((0,0)--(1,0)--(1,1)--(0,1)--(0,0)); draw((2,0)--(2,2)--(3,2)--(3,0)--(3,1)--(2,1)--(4,1)--(4,0)--(2,0)); draw((1,2)--(1,4)--(0,4)--(0,2)--(0,3)--(1,3)--(-1,3)--(-1,2)--(1,2)); draw((-1,1)--(-3,1)--(-3,0)--(-1,0)--(-2,0)--(-2,1)--(-2,-1)--(-1,-1)--(-1,1)); draw((0,-1)--(0,-3)--(1,-3)--(1,-1)--(1,-2)--(0,-2)--(2,-2)--(2,-1)--(0,-1)); size(100);
solution_text:
In fact only three configurations actually work, so \

Here's the way I did it. Though, now that I consider it, v_Enhance's method is much simpler: Let us divide the array into four subcategories: Center, Closest Squares, Furthest Squares, Other Squares. All of the colours must be the same in each category, and the distribution is $1, 4, 4, 4$, respectively. Thus, we must have a blue square in the center. The probability of this is $\frac{5}{13}$. For the remainder of the problem, we will deal only with blue squares. If they fit the pattern, then the two remaining categories will be filled with red squares. We don't really care where the next blue square goes, so let it be probability $\frac{12}{12} = 1$, which is negligible. After that, we have $11$ remaining squares and $3$ remaining blue squares. The category that we have chosen also has $3$ spaces, so we have probability $\frac{3}{11}$, $\frac{2}{10} = \frac{1}{5}$, and $\frac{1}{9}$. Multiplying everything together, we get \ Thus, we have answer $n = \boxed{429}$ DISCLAIMER: I didn't take the AIME, but now that I'm seeing the problems, I really wish I had.
